---
title: "Data Security & Classification Deep-Dive"
---

::: {.topic-badges}
[Novice]{.level-badge .level-novice} · **Governance, Ethics & Equity** track · ~1 hour, self-paced
:::

[← Back to the Governance, Ethics & Equity track](governance-ethics.qmd){.track-back}

*The rule under every other topic: the right tool for the data's sensitivity.*

## What you'll be able to do

- Classify data by sensitivity (public → high-risk)
- Match each tool to Drexel's approved classification
- De-identify or substitute when a tool isn't cleared

## Overview

Every AI decision starts with one question: how sensitive is this data, and is this tool approved for it? Drexel publishes which tools may be used with which data levels. Identifiable records, PHI, protected student data, and contract material have strict limits.

When a tool isn't cleared for your data, the move is to de-identify, use synthetic data, or switch to an approved tool — not to make an exception.

## Practice activities

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 1 · Novice — Classify and match</summary>
```

**Time** ~15 min · **Tools** Drexel AI Tools page + ChatGPT Edu

**Goal.** Map your data to the right tools.

**Setup.** List three data types you actually work with.

**Steps.**

1. For each, note its sensitivity (public → high-risk) and which Drexel-approved tools allow it (check the AI Tools page).

2. Sanity-check with AI:

   > I have these data types and these tools available. Which uses are appropriate and which aren’t, based on data sensitivity? [types] [tools]

3. Flag any current habit that doesn’t match.

**Expected result.** A data-to-tool matrix and a list of habits to change.

**Check your work.** Verify against Drexel’s official AI Tools page, not the AI’s guess.

**Common pitfalls.** “It’s probably fine” isn’t a classification. When unsure, treat data as more sensitive.

**Stretch (optional).** Write your team’s one-page “what goes where” cheat sheet.

```{=html}
</details>
```

```{=html}
<details class="activity"><summary>Activity 2 · Novice — Substitute safely</summary>
```

**Time** ~12 min · **Tools** ChatGPT Edu (fabricated data only)

**Goal.** Make sensitive data safe for a given tool.

**Setup.** A fabricated sensitive example.

**Steps.**

1. De-identify it:

   > Here is a record with identifiers. Produce a de-identified version safe for a Low-Risk tool, and list what you removed or generalized. [fabricated record]

2. Check: could the remaining fields still identify someone in combination?

3. Decide whether de-identification is enough or you need an approved tool.

**Expected result.** A de-identified version plus a re-identification check.

**Check your work.** Quasi-identifiers (age + ZIP + date) can re-identify — scrutinize them.

**Common pitfalls.** Removing names isn’t de-identification; combinations matter.

**Stretch (optional).** Try generating fully synthetic data instead (see the Methods track).

```{=html}
</details>
```


## Check your readiness

Answer these, then check — your score suggests whether to dive in or skim the readings first.

<div class="readiness-quiz">
<script type="application/json" class="quiz-data">
{"questions": [{"q": "The first question before using any AI tool is…", "options": ["Is this tool approved for this data's sensitivity?", "Is it free?", "Is it popular?", "Is it fast?"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Match the tool to the data classification."}, {"q": "If a tool isn't cleared for your data, you should…", "options": ["De-identify, use synthetic data, or switch tools", "Use it anyway", "Ask the AI to keep it secret", "Email it instead"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Don't make exceptions — substitute or switch."}, {"q": "Where do you confirm a tool's approved data level?", "options": ["Drexel's AI Tools page", "A web search", "The tool's ads", "A colleague's guess"], "answer": 0, "explain": "Use Drexel's official classification."}]}
</script>
</div>

## Recommended readings

Available in the shared OneDrive folder **Staff Faculty AI Workshop → Readings**, and online where linked:

- [**LLMs Can Unmask Pseudonymous Users at Scale (Ars Technica)**](https://arstechnica.com/security/2026/03/llms-can-unmask-pseudonymous-users-at-scale-with-surprising-accuracy/) — why de-identification is harder than it looks.
- [**AI and Humans: Making the Relationship Work (Schneier on Security)**](https://www.schneier.com/blog/archives/2026/01/ai-humans-making-the-relationship-work.html) — security thinking for AI use.

## Useful resources

- [Drexel AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools) — approved tools by data classification.
- [HHS De-identification Guidance](https://www.hhs.gov/hipaa/for-professionals/privacy/special-topics/de-identification/index.html) — HIPAA de-identification standards.

::: {.callout-warning}
## Data rules
This **is** the data-rules topic: never exceed a tool's approved classification. See [Tools & Access](../tools.qmd) and Drexel's [AI Tools page](https://drexel.edu/provost/ai/tools).
:::
